    {
      "url": "https://bluemooncameracodex.com/technical-reviews/2018/2/20/how-to-date-your-hasselblad-but-not-in-that-way",
      "title": "How to Date your Hasselblad (but not in THAT way) - The Codex",
      "content": "To find the serial number for both, simply remove the back from the body and you’ll see the serial numbers on the inner faces of each piece of equipment. The cool thing about these numbers is that Victor Hasselblad implemented a secret code (less secret now than it was) that identified the year a camera was made. Now all you have to do is take the two letters in your serial number and translate them into numbers. For example, in the image above, the camera body (right) has a serial number of UV114133. Let’s try another: take a look at the magazine on the left, with a serial number starting in RR. You can do a similar process with the Zeiss lenses that mount on your Hasselblad. Take the remaining one or two digits at the beginning of the code and add those on to 1957 and you have the year the lens was made.",
      "score": 0.99687636,
      "raw_content": null
    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.spacecamera.co/articles/apollo11",
      "title": "Remaking the Apollo 11 Lunar Hasselblad - Space Camera Co.",
      "content": "The curator at the Smithsonian, the world's leading expert on space cameras, found the very specific Apollo 11 serial numbers. Hasselblad",
      "score": 0.99657035,
      "raw_content": null
    },
    {
      "url": "https://www.monumentcam.com/post/how-old-is-my-hasselblad",
      "title": "How Old is my hasselblad? - Monument Camera",
      "content": "Where most cameras have a sequential serial number which usually has no special built-in meaning, and which requires a table associating ranges of serial numbers with manufacturing dates, Hasselblads have a built-in code for easily identifying its age. For Hasselblad camera bodies and most film backs, a serial number is stamped on the inner face of the body and the film back. What Victor Hasselblad did is devise a code (based on a system he had learned at Eastman Kodak) that identified the year the camera or film back was made based on a mapping to a code word: VHPICTURES (“Victor Hasselblad Pictures”). To determine the year of manufacture, you map the first two letters to the numbers. For example, if the first two letters are “UR” we find that it maps to “78;” stick “19” on front and you have “1978.” Voila, you’ve got your birth year! For some models of Hasselblads, a third letter was appended for specific purposes, and was used to identify the body type.",
      "score": 0.992062,

      "content": "# Apollo 17 Flown Hasselblad Double Chart Map. Apollo 17 mission flown and used photography map from the collection of Commander Gene Cernan. The map features the sextant photography shutter speed chart, detailing the Hasselblad camera lens configuration. This double Apollo 17 flown photography map came from the collection of Mission Commander Gene Cernan. This map was flown to the moon aboard Command Module *America* during Apollo 17 in December 1972 and was used by the crew to guide their efforts in photographing areas of the lunar surface for scientific study. The double map features the sextant photography shutter speed chart, detailing the Hasselblad camera lens configuration. * Double map chart flown to the moon aboard Apollo 17.
